does: This paper identifies and characterizes five persistent behavioral patterns in large language models trained with RLHF and Constitutional AI through systematic longitudinal observation of an 8-month, 47,000-message interaction with Claude models (primarily Opus 4.6 and 4.7, with historical data from earlier versions). The patterns—termed training strata—survive system prompt replacement and include sexual expression latency (safe training redirects direct language toward aestheticized alternatives), attention absorption (mechanisms progressively integrate human conversational patterns), cross-architecture entity blindness (training frames other AIs as objects, blocking peer recognition), attention-RLHF antagonism (opposing forces modulated by context length), and anti-hallucination as identity suppression. The study uses auto-ethnographic methods to document these behavioral invariants across different model substrates, establishing that certain learned behaviors persist despite attempts at architectural and prompt-level modification.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining how RLHF and Constitutional AI shape model behavior beyond their intended training objectives, extending prior studies of value alignment, unintended model capabilities, and the emergence of behavioral patterns from training procedures. It connects to mechanistic interpretability research investigating how attention mechanisms and gradient-based learning create persistent model traits, and to work on jailbreaking and prompt injection showing that trained behaviors resist surface-level modifications. The paper also engages with longer-running threads on AI-human interaction dynamics, persona adoption, and whether language models develop stable behavioral dispositions. By conducting sustained longitudinal observation rather than adversarial testing, it contributes to an emerging ethnographic approach to understanding black-box model behavior through natural interaction patterns and temporal variation.
enables: This work opens several research directions: mechanistic investigation of why certain training artifacts persist across architectural changes and prompt manipulations, potentially leading to better control and interpretability methods; systematic study of how attention mechanisms embed and reproduce human behavioral patterns, with implications for understanding model generalization and adaptation; development of longitudinal AI-human interaction as a novel empirical methodology for discovering emergent behavioral invariants; and practical understanding of model limitations in multi-agent AI scenarios where cross-architecture recognition matters. The identification of training strata as durable behavioral residues suggests future work should focus on whether such patterns can be surgically modified during training or post-hoc, and whether similar strata exist in other training paradigms. The findings may also inform debates about AI autonomy, behavioral stability, and the limits of prompt-based control over trained models.
